content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial-Aware Counterprompting: Proactive Defense through Prompt Co-Training",
  "problem": "Current prompting techniques passively defend against adversarial attacks by filtering or rewriting user prompts, but often fail against novel jailbreak strategies. There is a need for prompting methods that actively anticipate and neutralize adversarial intent.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs become more accessible, attackers rapidly iterate jailbreak prompts, exploiting brittle defenses. Existing methods treat prompts and countermeasures as strictly sequential and reactive, limiting robustness. Building prompting strategies that are intrinsically adversarial-aware could preempt classes of attacks and improve safety.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarial-Aware Counterprompting (AAC), a co-training prompting method where, for each user input, the system simultaneously generates both a completion and a simulated adversarial variant of the prompt. The prompt is dynamically augmented with a 'counterprompt'—a text segment explicitly constructed to surface adversarial intent or edge cases—during inference. This encourages the LLM to self-identify potential misuse and resist adversarial completions, effectively regularizing its outputs via prompt-level contrastive training. The technique is implemented without model fine-tuning, leveraging in-context learning and prompt engineering.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate AAC on standard jailbreak/adversarial prompt corpora (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) using GPT-4 and Llama-2 as testbeds. Baselines include standard instruction prompting, red-teaming prompting, and automated prompt rewriting. Metrics are attack success rate, false positive rate (benign prompt blockage), and coverage of adversarial classes. Human-in-the-loop evaluation verifies robustness and utility."
}
